fields.paper1_relevance_to_harness_design: core agent capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory), application-specific benchmarks (web, SWE, scientific, conversational), generalist agent platforms (AgentBench, HAL, Harbor, Exgentic), core benchmark dimensions (metrics, static vs dynamic environments, safety/robustness), and developer evaluation frameworks that integrate with the development cycle
fields.paper2_relevance_to_harness_design: two taxonomies: what to evaluate (task completion, response quality, user experience, memory/context, planning/tool integration) and how to evaluate (annotation-based, automated metrics, hybrid human-plus-quantitative, LLM self-judging), plus multi-step tool selection reasoning (MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench) and end-to-end multi-turn interaction evaluation

markdown:
1. Paper 1 (A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents) is directly relevant to designing an agent evaluation harness through its coverage of core agentic capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory), application-specific benchmarks (web, SWE, scientific, conversational agents), generalist agent evaluation platforms (AgentBench, HAL, Harbor, Exgentic), core benchmark dimensions (metrics, static vs dynamic environments, safety/robustness including pass^k), and evaluation frameworks for agent developers that integrate with the development environment and support evaluation across the development cycle. [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8][9]
2. Paper 2 (Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey) is directly relevant to designing an agent evaluation harness in the conversational setting through its two taxonomies: a 'what to evaluate' taxonomy covering task completion, response quality, user experience, memory/context retention, and planning/tool integration, and a 'how to evaluate' taxonomy covering annotation-based evaluation, automated metrics, hybrid human-plus-quantitative strategies, and LLM self-judging, along with multi-step tool selection and reasoning evaluation (e.g., MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench) and end-to-end multi-turn interaction evaluation. [10][11][12][13][14][15][16][17]
3. Read paper 1 (A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents) first when designing an agent evaluation harness for general/multi-application agents. It offers broader coverage spanning core agentic capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory), application-specific benchmarks (web, SWE, scientific, conversational agents), generalist agent evaluation platforms (AgentBench, HAL, Harbor, Exgentic), core benchmark dimensions (metrics, static vs dynamic environments, safety/robustness via pass^k), and developer evaluation frameworks that integrate with the development environment and support evaluation across the development cycle, whereas paper 2 is narrower in scope (multi-turn conversational settings); paper 2's deeper taxonomy for multi-turn dialogue can then be consulted afterward for conversational-agent components. [1][2][3][10][11][5][7][8][9][14]
